title: Infrastructure as Code
description:
## Defining Infrastructure in Files, Not Clicks

**Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** means describing your servers, networks, databases, and other cloud resources in **version-controlled text files** instead of clicking through a web console. The files become the single source of truth: reviewed in pull requests, diffed in Git, and applied repeatably across environments.

### Why it matters

| Manual console clicking | Infrastructure as Code |
|---|---|
| Undocumented, lives only in someone's memory | Codified, readable, in version control |
| Hard to reproduce a second environment | Re-apply the same files for dev/staging/prod |
| Drift creeps in silently | Changes are reviewed and diffed before applying |
| Error-prone manual steps | Automated, repeatable, auditable |

### Declarative vs imperative

- **Declarative** — you describe the *desired end state* ("I want 3 web servers and a load balancer") and the tool figures out how to get there. Terraform and AWS CloudFormation work this way.
- **Imperative** — you write the *step-by-step commands* to create resources ("run this, then that"). A hand-written shell script using a cloud CLI is imperative.

Declarative is preferred for infrastructure because the tool can compute the difference between what exists and what you want, and only change what's needed.

### The role of state

Declarative tools keep a **state file** that records which real resources they manage and their current attributes. State is how the tool maps your config to actual cloud resources, detects drift, and knows what to create, update, or destroy. Losing or corrupting state is dangerous, so teams store it remotely (e.g. an S3 bucket with locking) rather than on one laptop.

### The plan -> apply workflow

The core IaC loop is **preview, then commit**:

```text
  terraform plan    ->  shows a diff: what will be added / changed / destroyed
  (review it)
  terraform apply   ->  makes the real changes and updates state
```

This preview step is a safety net — you see exactly what will happen before any resource is touched.

### A short HCL example (Terraform)

```hcl
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0abcd1234"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  tags = {
    Name = "web-server"
  }
}
```

This declares *one* EC2 instance. Running `terraform apply` creates it; running again with no changes does nothing because the desired state already matches reality.

### The tool landscape

| Tool | Notes |
|---|---|
| **Terraform** | Cloud-agnostic, declarative, uses HCL; huge provider ecosystem. |
| **AWS CloudFormation** | AWS-native declarative IaC using JSON/YAML templates. |
| **Pulumi** | IaC using real programming languages (TypeScript, Python, Go). |
| **AWS CDK** | Define AWS infra in code; synthesizes to CloudFormation. |

All share the same goal: make infrastructure reproducible, reviewable, and automated.
